pi_items.source: NACA 4-digit naming convention verified via Wikipedia and aeronautical sources. First digit (2) = 2% max camber. Second digit (4) = 40% camber location. Last two digits (12) = 12% max thickness.
NACA 4-digit naming convention verified via Wikipedia and aeronautical sources. First digit (4) = 4% max camber. Second digit (4) = 40% camber location. Last two digits (24) = 24% max thickness.
Calculated from 50% interpolation of NACA 2412 (12%) and NACA 4424 (24%): (0.12 + 0.24)/2 = 0.18 = 18%. Expected interpolated thickness of 18% falls within range [15%, 21%].
Calculated from 50% interpolation of NACA 2412 (12%) and NACA 4424 (24%): (0.12 + 0.24)/2 = 0.18 = 18%. Expected interpolated thickness of 18% falls within range [15%, 21%].
Calculated from 50% interpolation of NACA 2412 (2%) and NACA 4424 (4%): (0.02 + 0.04)/2 = 0.03 = 3%. Expected interpolated camber of 3% falls within range [2%, 4%].
Calculated from 50% interpolation of NACA 2412 (2%) and NACA 4424 (4%): (0.02 + 0.04)/2 = 0.03 = 3%. Expected interpolated camber of 3% falls within range [2%, 4%].
Verified in verifier.py line 94-95: checks that 'len(coords) >= min_points' where min_points defaults to 30. This is a standard and reasonable minimum for airfoil coordinate definition.
Verified in setup_task.sh line 11 (directory creation), export_result.sh line 11 (output path definition), and task.json description. Path is consistent across all task scripts.

privileged_info_summary: The task involves interpolating two standard NACA airfoils: NACA 2412 (2% camber, 12% thickness) and NACA 4424 (4% camber, 24% thickness). A 50% linear blend produces expected values of 3% camber and 18% thickness, both within the metadata tolerance ranges (camber: 2-4%, thickness: 15-21%). The output must contain at least 30 coordinate points in Selig format and be exported to /home/ga/Documents/airfoils/interpolated_naca_50pct.dat. All airfoil data is synthetically generated by QBlade using NACA mathematical definitions.
